summary:
Prithviraj Chauhan (c. 1149–1192 AD) was the last great Hindu emperor of northern India, ruler of the Chahamana (Chauhan) dynasty who controlled the strategically vital region of Ajmer and Delhi, and whose defeat and death at the [[event:3527|Second Battle of Tarain]] in 1192 marked the decisive end of Hindu political dominance in the northern Indian plains and the beginning of Muslim rule under the [[event:2253|Delhi Sultanate]]. A celebrated warrior-king, Prithviraj is remembered in Indian tradition primarily through the epic poem Prithviraj Raso, composed by his court poet Chand Bardai, which celebrates his martial exploits and doomed romance with Sanyogita, princess of Kannauj — though the poem is a later, largely legendary composition that significantly embellishes historical events.

Prithviraj's historical significance lies primarily in his contest with Muhammad of Ghor (Mu'izz ad-Din Muhammad), the Ghurid sultan who led repeated invasions from Afghanistan into northern India. At the [[event:3527|First Battle of Tarain]] (1191), Prithviraj won a rare victory against the Ghurid forces, reportedly capturing Muhammad before releasing him. The following year at the Second Battle of Tarain (1192), Muhammad returned with a larger, better-organized force. The Rajput coalition under Prithviraj was decisively defeated; Prithviraj was captured and subsequently executed. The battle opened the Gangetic plain to Ghurid conquest: within a decade, Muhammad's general Qutb ud-Din Aibak had seized Delhi, establishing the administrative foundation for what would become the Delhi Sultanate. Prithviraj became a central figure in Rajput historical memory as the heroic last defender of Hindu India against Muslim conquest.
